findings.title: Encrypted setup container blocks pre-deployment security review
findings.description: The setup artifact is an OpenSSL password-encrypted blob, which prevents direct inspection of installer logic, package type, embedded scripts, or trustworthiness before decryption.
findings.evidence: Observed file type `openssl enc'd data with salted password`; `Salted__` header present; no plaintext archive signature visible before decryption.
findings.impact: Operators are forced to make trust decisions before they can inspect what will actually run, increasing supply-chain and deployment risk.
findings.remediation: Require a decrypt-then-review workflow in an isolated analysis directory before installation or execution on Raspberry Pi targets.
findings.hardening: Treat encrypted setup blobs as opaque containers, not trusted installers. Contents must be reviewed before deployment.

findings.title: Encrypted artifact lacks companion integrity or provenance metadata
findings.description: The provided encrypted artifact was not accompanied by a detached signature, signed manifest, or independent checksum package in the current evidence set.
findings.evidence: Enumeration of `engagements/playerv2-artifacts/` found the encrypted file and quick-scan notes, but no detached trust metadata or release manifest alongside the blob.
findings.impact: A tampered or swapped encrypted installer could be distributed without easy verification of provenance or expected contents.
findings.remediation: Ship encrypted setup blobs with detached signatures and independently verifiable release metadata.
findings.hardening: Separate confidentiality from integrity. Encryption should not be the only protection around installer trust.

findings.title: Related Player ecosystem demonstrates hardware-derived key reuse risk
findings.description: Adjacent Player research in the same environment shows secure storage previously derived decryption directly from Raspberry Pi serial values embedded in scripts. This creates a credible key-reuse and trust-model risk for other protected setup artifacts unless independently disproven.
findings.evidence: Workspace research under `research/ntv-hardware-lock/` and prior `player-vault` reporting documents serial-derived decryption via `hardware_lock.py` and `unlock_vault.py`, including validated serial `ffb6d42807368154` for the earlier vault flow.
findings.impact: If similar derivation or reuse is applied elsewhere in the setup pipeline, attackers with device-level knowledge may derive or predict protected deployment secrets more easily than intended.
findings.remediation: Audit `setup.enc` generation workflow to confirm whether any hardware-derived or reused passphrase logic exists. Rotate to unique per-artifact secrets or managed key material if reuse is present.
findings.hardening: Move encryption key management out of recoverable scripts and device-derived values; use explicit secret custody and release verification instead.

enhancements.recommendation: Decrypt encrypted installer artifacts only inside a dedicated analysis directory and review contents before any install-time execution.
Add detached signatures, signed manifests, and externally verifiable checksums to every protected setup blob.
Review whether `setup.enc` uses device-derived, reused, or script-recoverable decryption material, and eliminate that pattern if found.
